questions.question: What is the goal of a lightning trade-off round?
options: Make a clear decision tied to a priority, then say what the decision costs.
List every possible alternative without picking one.
Avoid mentioning downsides to sound confident.
Memorize one best practice for every system.
questions.explanation: Strong trade-off communication is about choosing and owning the cost of the choice.

questions.question: Why should a trade-off answer start from a requirement rather than from technology names?
options: Because the correct option depends on which user or operational outcome matters most.
Because distributed systems have no reusable patterns.
Because technologies never influence trade-offs.
Because interviewers prefer generic answers.
questions.explanation: Requirements determine what counts as a good choice.

questions.question: Why is naming one rejected alternative useful?
options: It shows that the decision was comparative and grounded, not just a memorized preference.
It replaces the need for metrics.
It guarantees the chosen option is optimal forever.
It matters only when discussing databases.
questions.explanation: Rejected alternatives make the trade-off structure visible.

questions.question: Which signal best proves that a trade-off answer is operationally grounded?
options: A metric or failure mode that would reveal the chosen option is hurting the system.
Only a long list of buzzwords.
The number of whiteboard boxes drawn.
A vendor comparison table with no workload context.
questions.explanation: A measurable consequence turns a verbal opinion into a falsifiable design choice.

questions.question: What is the main trade-off of forcing an early decision in a short round?
options: The answer becomes clearer and more senior-sounding, but you have less room for broad exploration.
It removes all risk of choosing poorly.
It makes workload shape irrelevant.
It eliminates the need to say what gets worse.
questions.explanation: Fast decisions improve pacing, but they require disciplined prioritization.

questions.question: If a trade-off answer does not mention what got worse, what is missing?
options: The real cost of the decision, which is exactly what makes the trade-off credible.
Only some optional polish.
Nothing important if the chosen architecture is modern.
A second diagram, but not reasoning.
questions.explanation: Trade-offs are persuasive because they acknowledge what was sacrificed.

questions.question: How should the answer change when the same decision applies only to a premium traffic class?
options: Re-evaluate the choice with that segment's latency, cost, and reliability goals instead of assuming one policy fits all traffic.
Use the exact same answer because segmentation never matters.
Ignore cost because premium traffic has no budget.
Remove observability because smaller scope is easier.
questions.explanation: Segmentation often changes the right answer materially.

questions.question: Why are short trade-off drills good preparation for full mocks?
options: They train you to state priorities, choose deliberately, and close strongly under time pressure.
They remove the need for sizing practice.
They matter only for wrap-up, not deep dives.
They ensure the interviewer will agree with your design.
questions.explanation: Trade-off clarity is one of the fastest ways to improve interview signal.
